The attractive character. This is kind of, now you kind of figure out, this is the market I'm in. I know who I want to serve. I'm looking for people who are going to be my dream clients. The next thing is coming back to you now. And we're going to go a lot deeper, obviously, into this. But I want to spend a little bit here because this is a big part of the process of understanding this and being okay with this part of becoming the attractive character, the charismatic leader, whatever you want to call it. The first time I ever heard this concept, I was hanging out with John Alliness. He was speaking at an event. And John, if you ever know him, he's a dude that teaches people, teaches guys how to pick up chicks. Like that's the market he's in. And his sub market, there's relationships, how to pick up chicks. And his was like, how to get women to come to you. Like is that a good, for all the men, just sit there, ladies will come to you. Don't do anything, let's come to you. That's his, he's made a lot of money with it, right? Because that was his whole market. And he talked about it. He said, what I teach my men to do over here is they have to learn how to become an attractive character. That's what women want. They want people who have charisma. They don't have to be a good looking, but you have to have presence, like these kind of things. And he taught, he teaches men how to do that. So he's at stages in the business event. He's like, just so you guys know, stuff I teach my men, how to get ladies to come to them is the same stuff you all need to know about how to get your customers to come to you. It's the same stuff. Okay. And he said, I have my men creating attractive character. And he taught this whole thing. And I was like, this is brilliant. Like when I started getting that, I was like, that's, that's like the key to this, that unlocks this whole thing. And so this is, man, probably 12 years ago, I started learning about this concept. So I started kind of testing it in little things. I'd be on stage speaking and I was like, okay, I'm going to tell my story because I was a wrestler at the time when I was kind of speaking things. So I'd talk about wrestling and talk about some of my things I do. And as I noticed, I would do my thing and I'd get to the back of the room and like the people that run back and buy were always like male athletes. They were like, hey, man, I played football wherever. Hey, I was a wrestler. Hey, I play hockey. Like, oh, and they're all the dudes that bought. And I was like, cool, like this is that kind of work. It was interesting. And then I was speaking at another event. In fact, I was telling Steven this other day, I got on stage and it was probably nine or 10 years ago. And I give this presentation and I did good. I like had a big table rush and I was like, yes, this is awesome. And then the speaker right after me was Allie Brown. So me and me, no Allie. And she got up and she gave almost the same presentation to me. And I was like, oh man, I felt so bad for her. I was like, didn't the promoter know like our topics were similar? Like, she should put them at least like separate days, but she spoke immediately after me. And I just like, and send them back to them feeling sick in my stomach for her. I'm like, man, this is so bad. Like, everybody bought my thing. Like no one's going to buy her. It's going to be so embarrassing for her. So she does a little pitch and she gets the end and all of a sudden I start seeing all the women popping up, boom, boom, boom, running to the back. And she closed every woman in the room almost like really close and she actually beat me in sales that day. I was like, so interesting. Like my offer was better, my presentation, like everything I thought, you know, I thought was better. And I'd already taken the money out of the room and she's still out closing me. It was insane. And I was like, God, there's something to this. And then one time I was speaking again, these are all my lessons from the stage. Like I learned, do you learn a lot when you're in front of people, when you just start talking, right? And sometimes when you're on stage talking, all of a sudden like something starts coming out and you're like, oh crap, like I shouldn't tell a story, right? And then it's too late because people are like, well, you have to keep going with it. So I'm on stage and my wife and I had just gone through fertility, trying to get pregnant, all these, the whole story, right? And I start talking. And all of a sudden like that story starts coming out. I'm like, oh no, like that's private. We don't share that like, but it's like coming out of my mouth and I'm like, I don't know what to do. I can't stop it. So I tell this whole story and I start crying on stage and it's like, ugh. And I do my thing and then the end I sell, I sell, right? And I go to the back of the room and it's cool. Like all the athletes were there. But then there was a whole different segment. They were like mothers, women, a whole different audience that was there. And I was like, it was so fascinating. And all these people are like, I have twins. I don't want to tell me their twin story and then they signed up. They want, and it was weird how like different parts of my person I put out there attracted different people into my world. And so we understand that's like, okay, now we know the pattern. Like how do we, how do we, like what things should we share? And obviously I don't share everything with my life. Like I'm very selective in what I do and I don't share, but I put things out specifically for reasons to get different people to come in and to be, to be part of it. And so that's kind of this whole track to characters about it's like figuring, figuring that out. And we're going to go a lot deeper in this over the next three days, but just a couple of quick things. Like number one, every good attractive character has to have a good backstory. I think it's tomorrow we're going to go really deep into story, which I'm so excited. I've never taught this stuff and I teach tomorrow. I'm just like crazy excited. So we're going to go deep into backstory. But the biggest thing to understand is that each of you guys as an expert, like the rest of the world looks at you and like you're on this hill and they're like, wow, like look what they did. Like they're amazing. For you to be relatable, for them to want to be, to become part of your world, you have to get off of your mountain, like hike back down where they're at, come back to where they're starting and be like, Hey man, I've been over there. It's awesome. But I was here too. Let me talk to you about that. And we tell the story here and we bring them with us. And that's what gets people to bond with you and have empathy with you and all the good stories telling stuff. We'll talk about tomorrow starts from that backstory. You getting off your pedestal, coming back down to them and having empathy and relating to them. And then they will follow you anywhere you want to go. Number two is attractive characters speak in parables, speak in stories, which is what all tomorrow is going to be about. We're going to get really good. You guys are going to have a chance to tell your stories and building out inventories of stories, a bunch of stuff tomorrow we're going to be doing. But it's getting really good at telling stories after story after story. We've been live for like an hour and a half so far. I don't know if I'm just keeping track. How many stories do I have told so far in the last hour and a half? Is that the real number? I don't know yet. But the more you get comfortable with that, the more you get comfortable with that. That's what gets people engaged and gets them not falling asleep at their desk. Third thing is character flaws. You have to be... This is so hard. You have to open those things up and share your flaws. I'm going to share a personal story about Natalie. She doesn't know yet. So I don't know Natalie super well. I met her yesterday as the first time person. But I've been stalking her and watching her, kind of like understand her and her business a little bit. And what's interesting is she super cares about it. I was like, what's... What caused people to follow her? And I went to one of your pages and you have your story. Do you want to talk? You want to tell that story? And I saw that and I was like, that's why people are in love with her. It was so good and so powerful. And it was sharing a character flaw. Yeah. So I am involved in the health and fitness industry and I kind of fell into it by accident. But where my social media falling just exploded was when I talked about this concept of... Perfectly imperfect. So after I had two kids and they were 10 pound babies, like my stomach was way, way, way out here. I gained 70 pounds and after I had them, after I had my daughter, I remember looking in the mirror and being like, my body is so different than I used to be. In my head, I thought I was an athlete, but I remember looking in the mirror and being like, why doesn't anybody talk about this? Like nobody told me that I was going to come home and still look six months pregnant. And like nobody told me. I was like kind of mad actually. So I flipped my camera out and I bent over and I showed that I have really like a lot... I'm in really good shape, but I have a lot of stretch skin on my stomach. And I posted that and I was like so scared. I wish like to click the button, my hand was like, oh my gosh, this is going to be awful. Everybody's going to be like, oh my gosh, that's disgusting. And it was amazing what happened is that people just... It was like a magnet. People just flocked to it. And so what I did is I used that idea and I reached out to some other big people like Heidi Powell, people who have big followings. And I created like one video that went totally viral where I shared all these things. Like you might see me now with my hair perfectly done in makeup, but this is what I really look like, you know, with bags under my eyes. And I showed like, you might see this beautiful home, but what you don't see is... And I showed through my house like every single picture frame has a stock photo in it because I suck at decorating. Like I am not a decorator. I showed my skin. And I said, you might see these professional photographs and see that, you know, those beautiful six pack abs. But when I bend over and I showed like the really, really wrinkly skin, you know, I just went through like a whole list of things. And so people connected to that. All of a sudden people just said, oh my gosh, I feel that way too. And I'm not alone. And somebody gets me. And once like I connected on that level, it was like, I could... Anything I put out, people would buy because all of a sudden everybody said, oh my gosh, I'm not alone. Like the vulnerability, like the level of shame that surrounded some of those sensitive topics went way down. And people all of a sudden wanted to be a part of that. And so every single product that I've created since has been around authenticity, vulnerability, talking about things that nobody else will talk about. Like I just released an ebook. Like after you have a baby, I don't know anybody here is moms or is a man who has kids. But a lot of times there's pelvic floor damage that happens after you have a baby. And when women jump on the trampoline, they pee their pants. And nobody talks about it. Like every woman I know struggles with it. Nobody talks about it. So I just, like you said, I partnered, I'm not an expert in that area, but I partnered with a doctor who helped me co-write the program. We launched it on Monday and it's been wildly successful because again, it's something that nobody talks about. So going back to what you're saying, sorry, that took your time. That was really long. But what I've kept into is that when you're willing to like be really authentic, you can't, if you're too calculated about it, it doesn't work. You actually have to be authentic behind it and you're willing to be vulnerable and share things that like make your stomach turn a little bit. Like I on this launch, I literally peed my pants doing a workout on camera. I have a company called Della Recal Club where we provide daily at home videos. Before I did this program, literally the film was rolling the whole time and you can see on my gray shorts, the spot getting bigger and bigger. And I was like mortified to share those pictures. And guess what? I shared it and got like, it was just insane. People were like, oh my gosh, me too. So once I was able to tap into that, like being willing to put yourself out there on things that feel, here's the thing about vulnerability is that it feels, it makes you feel small and it feels scary, but to everybody else, it looks brave and it looks courageous. And so, yeah, That was awesome. Thank you. Yeah, thank you for sharing that. It's powerful. You think about superheroes, Superman, it's the most boring character on earth. And like, man, like I was expecting losing thousands of members during our outage and instead, nothing, like not a single thing. People are rallying behind us. So a couple of rules I want to go through attractive character. I'm learning how this whole thing works. Okay. First step is you have to live the life that your audience wishes they could live. Okay. People want to become like you. They want to do cool stuff. And most of us are all private. That's why I do Snapchat. Like I share my life. Somebody's awkward. My wife gets pissed every single time. I want to be able to, you know, do those kind of things and like having those things out there. But it comes back to like living a cool life, like doing cool stuff and letting people see that. Because that's that's a big, big part of it. Second thing, as a leader, you have to maintain absolute certainty. Satema was in the last inner circle group. He had a whole presentation on on self confidence. He said self confidence is for children. Like certainty is for men. Okay. If you look at any situation, whoever comes into a situation, the person who's most certain will always win. Okay. And if you're going to be the expert and be leading people, you can't be like, I'll wish you Washington. Oh, what do you think? Like you have to be certain in that thing. Okay. And so like having absolute certainty. Um, any leader has to have this. If you're kind of like, I got self confidence. Yeah, I think we can win. Like if you think you're going to win, you're not going to win. You have to be like, I know I'm going to win. Uh, Dan Gable, who's the greatest wrestler, wrestler and probably the greatest wrestling coach of all time, uh, those who follow wrestling, um, he, uh, he said he will not let one of his wrestlers go out on the mat unless he knows he's going to win. So if you come like, I think I'm going to win coach. He pulls you off the roster. You're not allowed to go. Like he only let people who knew they were going to win step out on the mat. And it's a big deal. Like, uh, I was in an event where a guy was on stage and he was trying to pitch a mastermind group and he's like, well, I'm going to do a mastermind. I'm thinking like, um, thinking, I feel like five grand or 10 grand. Like what do you guys like, how many, we have five grand, 10 grand. Okay. And then maybe I'm like, dude, like, I do not want to give you anything. If you came here, this is how it's going to work. Can you watch Garrett White step on stage and like this, that's going to work guys. You're going to pay me this much money and this is how you're going to do it. And this is when you're going to do it. You're like, all right, here you go. Right. Like absolute certainty wins in every situation. This is true in business, true in relationship, uh, everything, like absolute certainty wins. Okay. Uh, number three here is do not be boring. Oh, that's like, just don't be boring. Like, um, okay. This is my little design I designed to show this. This is my prolific index. Okay. This is how you know if you're boring and there's like the boring side and then there's like the crazy side. Okay. So like right here in the middle is the mainstream on the sides are crazy. And in the middle is prolific. This is where you make a bunch of money. Okay. That's why school's got to trick you and the whole bunch of other ways to get your money because they teach you crap. It's all in the mainstream, right? Then on the outsides of it are like the crazy zone. Okay. Um, so if you go out here, this is like, um, how do you guys see the movie? Eat the sun. This movie's actually really awesome. Am I the only one that's seen it? All right. Well, let me tell you about it. Anthony's seen it. Don't lie. Yes. Anthony's in on it. I was blind when I watched it. Anthony watched it. So somewhere between like the mainstream and the crazy is that it's a little spot in the middle. I call the prolific zone where like people make money. Okay. Um, so the weight loss space, uh, you know, Dave Astor's with my friends, like he, he had this idea that was kind of like a little crazy, but not quite too crazy. He's like, Hey, uh, I went on this and he had a story. We'll talk about the story time. Like I was on this hike in the Himalayan mountains and I got lost and I was cold. And I was hungry. I met these people and they took me inside and they had yak butter and they put inside this coffee and I drank it and that energy, my brain, and I couldn't figure it out. So I went back home and I realized that butter is good for you. And I started putting butter in my coffee. Now this is this huge thing and he called it bull proof. And now he's got this huge mass movement of people who put butter in their coffee. Okay. And it's, it's not, it's kind of crazy, but not quite like insane. And it's not really mainstream. It's in the middle here that gets traction. That gets media. That gets people listening to people talking. Um, and you look at Dave Ashby, like he's one of the worst salespeople I've ever seen that his, his, uh, everything he sells is just like, Hey guys, this is some weird thing I take. And he explains it in some super nerdy way. It has an order link and people buy it because that whole butter story, they, they're bought into that. Like that was his thing. And so for all you guys, it's like figuring out that piece where you're, you're not in the mainstream and you're not crazy. Cause if you understand that, like that's, that's the sweet spot where people give you a bunch of money. And the second thing is, is in the, in here, this is like the polarity as well. Um, if you look at something and you have this thing, right? And if you start becoming polar where there's like this, this polarization that happens, like in the polar zones is where, where people are, or we start making money. Okay. If you're neutral, like nobody cares. Okay. Uh, a lot of times we try like, we don't want to offend people. Like this whole political thing has been really hard. Cause if you notice like everyone's like so polar that it's like offending everyone. So there's a part you gotta be careful of. You get too polar, you slip into the crazy zone and then it's like, you just, you lose all your fans and your followers. But if you, if you do it right where you're in the middle here, um, where you, you have some polarity, you're, you're controversial. You're doing stuff where you have a stand and you're taking those things and you, you figure that zone. Like that's, that's where the magic happens. And so it's kind of figuring that out for you. Everyone's going to be different. Some people will go further. Um, you know, Garrett, why it's a close friend. Garrett goes way further and polar than I do, but I have some of that as well. Like it's kind of figuring out where you're going to fit in that and where you're comfortable with doing, but understanding that that's, that's where the money's made is in that section. It's not going to be neutral and being boring. It's not being insane. It's like right in, in the middle there. Okay. Don't be boring. The next thing I want to understand, I hope you understand is how to use persuasion and again, tomorrow's the story selling stuff. It's all going to be persuasion. Um, but there's a really cool thing that I want to share because it's going to go tie into what we're talking about. Um, it's the one sentence persuasion guide. Have any of you guys read this book? It's called the one sentence persuasion. And, uh, it's really short. You can read it like in, in like 15 minutes. Um, but here's the one sentence. I'll give you guys the cliff notes. Here's the one sentence. You guys ready? It says this, people will do anything for those who encourage their dreams, justify their failures, lie their fears, confirm their suspicions and help them throw rocks at their enemies. Like that's magic. Like this, this is like ever since I read this has been burned into my mind. I drew a little image, so I never forget it. And when I'm teaching and talking and I'm doing things, like I'm trying to weave this in all the time. In fact, when we started doing our webinars and our presentations, usually the first five minutes of my presentation, five minutes of the presentation I've done, I've touched on every, all five of these things. Okay. So I'm going to kind of break these down. Um, first one is encouraging their dreams. Okay. So I'm talking to someone like, Hey, I'm going to welcome you to the webinar. I'm going to welcome you to this telos seminar. I'm going to welcome you to this event, whatever it is. Like, and I know all you guys have a goal that you want to blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, and I want to tell you like it's possible. Like it's happened for me. It can happen for you as well. Okay. It's encouraging their dreams. Second, just find their failures. Um, but you know, my guess is probably not the first time you've ever been on a webinar or tell us them in our sales video, whatever it is, right? Uh, you probably struggled in the past, right? And I understand that like people fell out of time. It's not your fault. Like it's not that you weren't able to work because the system didn't work for you, right? Or the product you tried in the past or whatever the thing you were using, didn't work. So I'm just finding their failures. Okay. Number three is a lie of their fears. Um, this is a lie is a word I always like struggle with. It means to diminish or put to rest. So, um, you know, I'm a lot of you guys on this webinar and you're probably nervous, think I'm going to try to sell you something or you think I'm a scam, you know, or you've tried this stuff before and like, I'm going to say, like it's going to be okay. Like this is, this is like a normal thing and we got tons of people that have success with this. In fact, let me show you this really quick story about so and so, like it's happened, like it worked for them, like just hang out and don't like stress. Like watch this thing. And if it's good for you, then it'll be, you know, it'll be awesome. Uh, confirm the suspicions. You know, you probably think that, uh, here today, I'm going to be selling you something like you're right. Like if we got something that might be really good fit for you, uh, it could be good for, but even if you don't buy anything, this presentation is going to help you understand that, that, that, that, that help them throw rock at their enemies. Um, everyone of you guys hopefully have a common enemy that you, you fight in your business. For us right now, we're doing a whole big playoff VCs and stuff like that. And you'll see more and more of this cause all of our competitors are all VC backs like we're, I don't know how much we'll talk, we'll, we'll talk about this, but you'll see a lot of fun hiking live for those who were there. Um, like we're going after infusion soft lead page, like all of our competitors are VC back. Like we're, we're like making a big deal out of it. We're throwing rocks at our enemies and trying to unite our people against them. And like, and making that like, look, you can work with companies like us who we care about our customers or compare, comes like them who worry about their board of directors. Okay. Like you are our customers. They're customers, the board of directors. That's why we serve you guys and they serve other people. Like we're here for you guys. Like, so we're trying to figure out who your common enemies and throwing rocks at them. So I tried to do that at the very beginning of like, whatever I'm doing, I'm thinking through these things and weaving those things in, uh, because as soon as you do that, like you've one sentence persuasion, like you've got all those things in there within the first five minutes, like people like, oh, cool. Like, man, he's encouraging my dreams. Like, like I fell in the past, but it's okay. Again, I'm not freaked out. It's going to be good. And like, yeah, there are bad people out there, but this is a safe place. And like, yeah, those are the bad people. Like, let's throw right that quick. You've got them. Like there's a super fast thing. And so for all of you guys, what I want you to do, this will be the exercise I believe, um, we make sure that's the plan. Yeah. So what I want you to do is I want you guys to write out five sentences basically for your new opportunity, for your new market, the thing you're going to be selling, we're just basically going to do that. You're going to justify their failures, lie their fears, throw rocks at enemies, confront suspicion, encourage their dreams. So just writing out sentence for each one of those things. A lot of people like, this is cool, but it's going to take me 20 minutes to explain like, you can do it in one sentence. So each one of these things from one sentence. So five sentences. I want you guys to write. Let's come back to this. So that's, that's some stuff here on number one, the charismatic leader. And the biggest thing you guys with this is just like doing it and doing it and being open and being vulnerable. The, one of the big things from the last set of inter-circle meetings, which was kind of interesting. I didn't know a podcast after last semester, we talked about this, like, a lot of them like come thinking it's going to be like, all right, we're all going to focus on like, here's the tactic. But it, like, it was almost a hundred percent, like being open and being authentic and like being willing to share the stuff that's scary about sharing. And, um, it's, it's crazy how that's what brings people to you where it's like, we always want to be perfect because we don't want people coming. We want people to like us. We try to be perfect. And that's actually the thing that keeps people from liking us. It's like the weirdest thing. As we share and like kind of open up, that's when, that's when people, people come. Um, so.
